source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.2 LVM (pp. 130-133)

questions.q: What core problem of standard partitions does LVM solve?
options: Standard partitions have a fixed capacity once mounted; LVM lets disk capacity be adjusted flexibly
Standard partitions cannot hold a file system
Standard partitions cannot be mounted
Standard partitions are slower to read
questions.why: A standard partition's size is fixed once mounted; LVM adds flexibility (resize, online moves, snapshots, mirroring, striping).

questions.q: What is a PV (Physical Volume) in LVM?
options: The basic storage block - a disk or partition initialised for use by LVM
A group of several disks
A file system built on a volume group
The smallest allocation unit
questions.why: A PV is the basic LVM storage logic block, created from a disk or partition (or RAID array) after initialisation.

questions.q: What is the relationship between PV, VG and LV?
options: One or more PVs make up a VG; LVs are carved out of a VG and hold the file systems
One LV contains several VGs, each made of PVs
PV, VG and LV are three names for the same thing
A VG is built on top of an LV
questions.why: PVs are pooled into a Volume Group (VG); Logical Volumes (LVs) are then created from the VG, and file systems live on the LVs.

questions.q: What is a PE, and what is its default size?
options: The Physical Extent - the smallest allocatable unit in a PV, 4 MB by default
The Primary Entry - 512 bytes
The Partition Encoding - 1 MB
The Physical Endpoint - 4 KB
questions.why: The PE is the smallest allocatable unit of a PV (default 4 MB); the LE is its counterpart in an LV, and in one VG the PE and LE match one-to-one.

questions.q: What is the main drawback of LVM?
options: If one physical volume fails, all logical volumes using it are lost (so use LVM on RAID)
It cannot be resized
It only works on a single disk
It requires a GUI
questions.why: Losing one PV loses every LV that spans it, so LVM is best layered on RAID disks.

questions.q: Why must there be at least one non-LVM partition for booting?
options: LVM is managed by the operating system, so the BIOS needs a plain partition to boot from
The BIOS reads LVM natively but is slow
LVM partitions cannot hold /home
It is only a convention with no technical reason
questions.why: LVM is understood only by the OS, not the BIOS, so a non-LVM partition (typically /boot) is required to start.
